topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The beige cubicle walls seemed to press in on Mark. He tapped his pen against the laminate desk rhythmically, a sharp, quick sound that echoed in the otherwise quiet office space. Another meeting scheduled, another mandatory orientation session. He glanced at the calendar on his monitor, the dates for his new assignment already highlighted in a jarring shade of orange. "Just get it over with," he muttered under his breath, the words barely audible. He shoved his keyboard back into its resting place with unnecessary force, the click sounding louder than he intended. He could feel a tightening in his chest, a restless energy vibrating just beneath his skin, urging him toward the next thing, any thing, that would end this current waiting period.

[story 2]

"So, this is it?" Sarah asked, her voice bright and annoyingly cheerful as she stacked the final files. David sighed, running a hand over his closely cropped hair. He didn't look up from the stack, his jaw visibly clenched. The thought of the new department—logistics, apparently—felt like a physical weight settling onto his shoulders. He wanted to be gone. He wanted the tedious routine of his current role to dissolve into something more dynamic, something that demanded his full attention. He stood up abruptly, the chair scraping back on the floor with a grating noise. "Alright," he said, his tone clipped, "when do I start packing?"

[story 3]

Eleanor stared at the clock above the water cooler. The second hand moved with agonizing slowness. Every minute dragged like thick molasses. She fiddled with the strap of her messenger bag, the worn leather digging slightly into her shoulder. She re-read the transfer memo for the fifth time, her eyes scanning the unfamiliar jargon of the new division. A low hum of frustration built in her throat, a sensation she tried to smother with forced calm. "This can't just… happen," she thought, her knuckles whitening where she gripped the edge of the desk. She needed a clear directive, a concrete endpoint to this limbo. She pushed a stray strand of hair behind her ear, her movements jerky, signaling an internal desire for immediate forward motion.
